name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ ٱلْحَرْثِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ نَصِيبًا فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَهَـٰذَا لِشُرَكَآئِنَا ۖ فَمَا كَانَ لِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ فَلَا يَصِلُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ فَهُوَ يَصِلُ إِلَىٰ شُرَكَآئِهِمْ ۗ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (١٣٦)
```

```english
They dedicate a portion to Allah out of what He has created of the crops and cattle, and say, ‘This is for Allah,’ so do they maintain, ‘and this is for our partners.’ But what is for their partners does not reach Allah, and what is for Allah reaches their partners. Evil is the judgement that they make. (136)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَ لِكَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ قَتْلَ أَوْلَـٰدِهِمْ شُرَكَآؤُهُمْ لِيُرْدُوهُمْ وَلِيَلْبِسُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ دِينَهُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا فَعَلُوهُ ۖ فَذَرْهُمْ وَمَا يَفْتَرُونَ (١٣٧)
```

```english
That is how to most of the polytheists the slaying of their children is presented as decorous by those whom they ascribe as partners [to Allah], that they may ruin them and confound their religion for them. Had Allah wished, they would not have done it. So leave them with what they fabricate. (137)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَنْعَـٰمٌ وَحَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ لَّا يَطْعَمُهَآ إِلَّا مَن نَّشَآءُ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ حُرِّمَتْ ظُهُورُهَا وَأَنْعَـٰمٌ لَّا يَذْكُرُونَ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَيْهِ ۚ سَيَجْزِيهِم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (١٣٨)
```

```english
And they say, ‘These cattle and tillage are a taboo: none may eat them except whom we please,’ so they maintain, and there are cattle whose backs are forbidden and cattle over which they do not mention Allah’s Name, fabricating a lie against Him. Soon He will requite them for what they used to fabricate. (138)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ مَا فِى بُطُونِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ خَالِصَةٌ لِّذُكُورِنَا وَمُحَرَّمٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِنَا ۖ وَإِن يَكُن مَّيْتَةً فَهُمْ فِيهِ شُرَكَآءُ ۚ سَيَجْزِيهِمْ وَصْفَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٣٩)
```

```english
And they say, ‘That which is in the bellies of these cattle is exclusively for our males and forbidden to our wives. But if it be still-born, they will all share it.’ Soon He will requite them for their allegations. Indeed, He is all-wise, all-knowing. (139)
```

```arabic
قَدْ خَسِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَتَلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَهُمْ سَفَهًۢا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَحَرَّمُوا۟ مَا رَزَقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱفْتِرَآءً عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (١٤٠)
```

```english
They are certainly losers who slay their children foolishly without knowledge, and forbid what Allah has provided them, fabricating a lie against Allah. Certainly, they have gone astray and are not guided. (140)
```

```arabic
۞ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ جَنَّـٰتٍ مَّعْرُوشَـٰتٍ وَغَيْرَ مَعْرُوشَـٰتٍ وَٱلنَّخْلَ وَٱلزَّرْعَ مُخْتَلِفًا أُكُلُهُۥ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلرُّمَّانَ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَـٰبِهٍ ۚ كُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦٓ إِذَآ أَثْمَرَ وَءَاتُوا۟ حَقَّهُۥ يَوْمَ حَصَادِهِۦ ۖ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (١٤١)
```

```english
It is He who produces gardens trellised and without trellises, and palm-trees and crops of diverse produce, olives and pomegranates, similar and dissimilar. Eat of its fruits when it fructifies, and give its due on the day of harvest, and do not be wasteful; indeed, He does not like the wasteful. (141)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ حَمُولَةً وَفَرْشًا ۚ كُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٤٢)
```

```english
Of the cattle [some] are for burden and [some] for slaughter. Eat of what Allah has provided you and do not follow in Satan’s footsteps; he is indeed your manifest enemy. (142)
```

```arabic
ثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَزْوَٰجٍ ۖ مِّنَ ٱلضَّأْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمَعْزِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۗ قُلْ ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۖ نَبِّـُٔونِى بِعِلْمٍ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٤٣)
```

```english
Eight mates: two of sheep, and two of goats. Say, ‘Is it the two males that He has forbidden or the two females, or what is contained in the wombs of the two females? Inform me with knowledge, should you be truthful.’ (143)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلْإِبِلِ ٱثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۗ قُلْ ءَآلذَّكَرَيْنِ حَرَّمَ أَمِ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ أَمَّا ٱشْتَمَلَتْ عَلَيْهِ أَرْحَامُ ٱلْأُنثَيَيْنِ ۖ أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ وَصَّىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا ۚ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا لِّيُضِلَّ ٱلنَّاسَ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٤)
```

```english
And two of camels and two of oxen. Say, ‘Is it the two males that He has forbidden or the two females, or what is contained in the wombs of the two females? Were you witnesses when Allah enjoined this upon you?’ So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah to mislead the people without any knowledge? Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (144)
```

```arabic
قُل لَّآ أَجِدُ فِى مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ مُحَرَّمًا عَلَىٰ طَاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُۥٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً أَوْ دَمًا مَّسْفُوحًا أَوْ لَحْمَ خِنزِيرٍ فَإِنَّهُۥ رِجْسٌ أَوْ فِسْقًا أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٤٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘I do not find in what has been revealed to me that anyone be forbidden to eat anything except carrion or spilt blood, or the flesh of swine—for that is indeed unclean—or an impiety offered to other than Allah.’ But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or aggressive, indeed your Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (145)
```

```arabic
وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا كُلَّ ذِى ظُفُرٍ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْبَقَرِ وَٱلْغَنَمِ حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ شُحُومَهُمَآ إِلَّا مَا حَمَلَتْ ظُهُورُهُمَآ أَوِ ٱلْحَوَايَآ أَوْ مَا ٱخْتَلَطَ بِعَظْمٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِبَغْيِهِمْ ۖ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (١٤٦)
```

```english
To the Jews We forbade every animal having an undivided hoof, and of oxen and sheep We forbade them their fat, except what is borne by their backs or the entrails, or what is attached to the bones. We requited them with that for their rebelliousness, and We indeed speak the truth. (146)
```

```arabic
فَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُل رَّبُّكُمْ ذُو رَحْمَةٍ وَٰسِعَةٍ وَلَا يُرَدُّ بَأْسُهُۥ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٤٧)
```

```english
But if they deny you, say, ‘Your Lord is dispenser of an all-embracing mercy, but His punishment will not be averted from the guilty lot.’ (147)
```

```arabic
سَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَآ أَشْرَكْنَا وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ ذَاقُوا۟ بَأْسَنَا ۗ قُلْ هَلْ عِندَكُم مِّنْ عِلْمٍ فَتُخْرِجُوهُ لَنَآ ۖ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَخْرُصُونَ (١٤٨)
```

```english
The polytheists will say, ‘Had Allah wished we would not have ascribed any partner [to Him], nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything.’ Those who were before them had denied likewise until they tasted Our punishment. Say, ‘Do you have any [revealed] knowledge that you can produce before us? You follow nothing but conjectures, and you do nothing but surmise.’ (148)
```

```arabic
قُلْ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحُجَّةُ ٱلْبَـٰلِغَةُ ۖ فَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٤٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘To Allah belongs the conclusive argument. Had He wished, He would have surely guided you all.’ (149)
```

```arabic
قُلْ هَلُمَّ شُهَدَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَشْهَدُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَ هَـٰذَا ۖ فَإِن شَهِدُوا۟ فَلَا تَشْهَدْ مَعَهُمْ ۚ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَهُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يَعْدِلُونَ (١٥٠)
```

```english
Say, ‘Bring your witnesses who may testify that Allah has forbidden this.’ So if they testify, do not testify with them, and do not follow the desires of those who deny Our signs, and those who do not believe in the Hereafter and equate [others] with their Lord. (150)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses argue against the polytheists (المشركين) regarding several rulings concerning food and other matters that were prevalent among them, mentioning Allah’s ruling concerning these things.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ الْحَرْثِ وَالْأَنْعامِ نَصِيباً** — "They assign to Allah a share of the crops and livestock He has created" until the end of the verse. Creation (الذرء) refers to bringing into existence (الإيجاد) by way of origination (الاختراع); it seems its linguistic root implies manifestation (الظهور). Crops (الحرث) refers to plantations (الزرع).

His saying: **بِزَعْمِهِمْ** — "by their claim" in the phrase **فَقالُوا هذا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ** — "and they say, ‘This is for Allah,’ by their claim" is a form of exoneration (التنزيه), similar to His saying: **وَقالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ** — "And they say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!" (21:26). Claim (الزعم) refers to belief (الاعتقاد), and it is mostly used for that which does not correspond to reality.

His saying: **وَهذا لِشُرَكائِنا** — "and this is for our partners." He attributed the partners (الشركاء) to them because they were the ones who affirmed them and believed in them, similar to the phrases "the leaders of faithlessness" (أئمة الكفر), "their leaders," and "their guardians." It is also said that the partners were attributed to them because they used to set aside some of their wealth for them, thereby making them partners to themselves.

In any case, the combination of the two sentences—namely, His saying: **فَقالُوا هذا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَهذا لِشُرَكائِنا** — "and they say, ‘This is for Allah,’ by their claim, ‘and this is for our partners’"—is an elaboration (تفصيل) following a general statement (إجمال), explaining how they assigned a share to Allah from His creation. This serves as a preamble and preparation for deriving another ruling from it, which He mentions in His saying: **فَما كانَ لِشُرَكائِهِمْ فَلا يَصِلُ إِلَى اللهِ وَما كانَ لِلَّهِ فَهو يَصِلُ إِلى شُرَكائِهِمْ** — "What is for their partners does not reach Allah, but what is for Allah reaches their partners."

Since this ruling—despite being fundamentally void and a fabrication (افتراء) against Allah—is not free from disparaging the presence of the Exalted by favoring the side of the idols over His side, He condemned it by saying: **ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ** — "Evil is the judgment they make." The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَذلِكَ زَيَّنَ لِكَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ قَتْلَ أَوْلادِهِمْ شُرَكاؤُهُمْ** — "That is how their partners have made the killing of their children seem decorous to many of the polytheists" until the end of the verse. Those other than Ibn 'Amir read **زَيَّنَ** (made decorous) with a *fathah* on the *zay* as an active verb, and **قَتْلَ** (killing) with a *nasb* on the *lam* as the object of **زَيَّنَ**, which is then annexed to **أَوْلادِهِمْ** (their children) in the genitive case (*jarr*) as the object of the verbal noun "killing." **شُرَكاؤُهُمْ** (their partners) is the subject (*fa'il*) of **زَيَّنَ**.

The meaning is that the idols—due to the status they held in the hearts of the polytheists and the illusory love in their souls—made it seem decorous to many polytheists to kill their children and offer them as sacrifices (قرابين) to draw near to the deities, as recorded in the history of the ancient pagans and Sabaeans. This is distinct from the issue of infanticide (الوأد) practiced by the Banu Tamim among the Arabs, for the context of the verse refers to "children" (الأولاد) in general, not specifically daughters.

It is also said that "partners" (الشركاء) refers to the devils (الشياطين), or the servants of the idols, or the miscreants among people.

Ibn 'Amir read **زَيَّنَ** with a *dammah* on the *zay* as a passive verb, **قَتْلُ** with a *dammah* on the *lam* as the pro-agent (*na'ib al-fa'il*), and **أَوْلادَهُمْ** with a *nasb* as the object of the verbal noun "killing," which is separated from its annexed term. **شُرَكائِهِمْ** is in the genitive case as the annexed term and the subject of the verbal noun.

His saying, the Exalted: **لِيُرْدُوهُمْ وَلِيَلْبِسُوا عَلَيْهِمْ دِينَهُمْ** — "to ruin them and to confuse their religion for them." Ruination (الإرداء) means destruction (الإهلاك). What is meant is the destruction of the polytheists through their ingratitude (الكفر) for Allah’s blessings and their transgression against His creation, and the confounding of their religion by presenting falsehood in the guise of truth. Thus, the pronoun in "their children" (أَوْلادِهِمْ) in all three instances refers back to "many of the polytheists."

It is also said that ruination refers to destruction in the literal sense of killing, which would necessitate that the first pronoun refers to the children, while the second and third refer to the "many," or all of them refer to the polytheists through a certain figurative consideration (عناية). The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالُوا هذِهِ أَنْعامٌ وَحَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ** — "And they say, ‘These livestock and crops are a taboo’" until the end of the verse. Taboo (الحجر) with a *kasrah* on the *ha* means prohibition (المنع), and it is explained by the subsequent phrase: **لا يَطْعَمُها إِلَّا مَنْ نَشاءُ** — "none may eat them except whom we wish." That is, these livestock and crops are forbidden except for those we wish to permit. It is narrated that they used to offer them to their idols, and they did not permit their consumption except for the men who served their idols—excluding women—according to their claim.

His saying: **وَأَنْعامٌ حُرِّمَتْ ظُهُورُها** — "and livestock whose backs are forbidden." That is, they said: "These are livestock whose backs are forbidden," or "They have livestock whose backs are forbidden." These refer to the *sa'ibah*, *bahirah*, and *hami* which Allah negated in His saying: **ما جَعَلَ اللهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلا سائِبَةٍ وَلا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلا حامٍ وَلكِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ** — "Allah has not prescribed any bahirah, or sa’ibah, or wasilah, or ham; but those who are faithless fabricate lies against Allah, and most of them do not apply reason" (5:103). It is also said they are some of these, based on the previous disagreement regarding their meanings in the commentary on the verse of *al-Ma'idah*.

His saying: **وَأَنْعامٌ لا يَذْكُرُونَ اسْمَ اللهِ عَلَيْهَا** — "and livestock over which they do not mention Allah’s Name." That is, they have livestock over which they would invoke the names of idols instead of Allah’s Name. It is also said these were the animals they would not ride during the Hajj, or livestock over which they never mentioned Allah’s Name in any of their affairs. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالُوا ما فِي بُطُونِ هذِهِ الْأَنْعامِ خالِصَةٌ لِذُكُورِنا** — "And they say, ‘What is in the bellies of these livestock is exclusively for our males’" until the end of the verse. What is meant by "what is in the bellies" are the fetuses of the *bahirah* and *sa'ibah*. They used to permit them for men but not women if they were born alive; however, if born dead, both men and women would eat them. It is also said it refers to milk, or both fetuses and milk.

The meaning of His saying: **سَيَجْزِيهِمْ وَصْفَهُمْ** — "He will soon requite them for their description" is that He will requite them for the very act of their description, as it will return upon them as a consequence and punishment; this involves a type of figurative emphasis (عناية). It is also said the implied meaning is "He will requite them *because of* their description," or "He will requite them with the *punishment for* their description," where the annexed term is omitted. The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَدْ خَسِرَ الَّذِينَ قَتْلُوا أَوْلادَهُمْ سَفَهاً بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** — "They are certainly losers who kill their children foolishly, without knowledge" etc. This is a rebuttal to the fabricated rulings recounted from them in the previous verses, such as the killing of children and the prohibition of various types of livestock and crops. It states that this behavior of theirs is a loss (خسران) and a deviation (ضلال) devoid of guidance.

He described the killing of children as "foolishness without knowledge" (سفهاً بغير علم). Similarly, He substituted "livestock and crops" with "what Allah has provided them" (ما رزقهم الله) and described their prohibition as "fabrication against Allah" (افتراء على الله). This serves as an admonition and a rationale (تعليل) for their loss; it is as if it were said: They lost in killing their children because they acted foolishly without knowledge, and they lost in prohibiting types of livestock and crops as a fabrication against Allah, for these are from Allah’s provision, and far be it from the Exalted to provide them with something and then forbid it to them.

Then, the Exalted explained their deviation in prohibiting crops and livestock—despite them being part of Allah’s provision—in a detailed manner, arguing from the perspective of reason (العقل) and the welfare of the servants' livelihood in His saying: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَ جَنَّاتٍ** — "It is He who produced gardens" through the next four verses. Then, He argued from the perspective of revelation (السمع) in His saying: **قُلْ لا أَجِدُ فِي ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلى طاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ** — "Say, ‘I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden to an eater who eats it’" until the end of the verse.

The sum of these five verses is that their prohibition of types of crops and livestock is a deviation on their part, unsupported by any proof; neither reason and the consideration of human welfare point them toward it, nor does the revelation sent down by Allah, the Glorified, guide them to it. Thus, they are in a state of loss.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَ جَنَّاتٍ مَعْرُوشاتٍ وَغَيْرَ مَعْرُوشاتٍ** — "It is He who produced gardens, trellised and untrellised" — until His saying — **وَغَيْرَ مُتَشابِهٍ** — "and unlike." A trellised tree (الشجرة المعروشة) is one whose branches are raised upon supports, like a grapevine. The root of *al-'arsh* (العرش) is elevation (الرفع). Thus, trellised gardens are vineyards and the like, while untrellised gardens are those whose trees stand on their own trunks without supports.

His saying: **وَالزَّرْعَ مُخْتَلِفاً أُكُلُهُ** — "and crops of diverse produce" refers to what is eaten from them, such as grains like wheat, barley, lentils, and chickpeas.

His saying: **وَالزَّيْتُونَ وَالرُّمَّانَ مُتَشابِهاً وَغَيْرَ مُتَشابِهٍ** — "and olives and pomegranates, similar and unlike." That is, each of them has similar and dissimilar varieties, as suggested by the context. Similarity (التشابه) between two fruits lies in their being identical in taste, shape, color, or otherwise.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلُوا مِنْ ثَمَرِهِ إِذا أَثْمَرَ** — "Eat of its fruit when it fructifies" until the end of the verse. The command here denotes permissibility (الإباحة), as it occurs to lift a prohibition implied by the creation of gardens, palms, crops, and others. The context indicates that the implied meaning is: It is He who produced gardens, palms, and crops, etc., and He commanded you to eat of their fruit and commanded you to give its due on the day of its harvest, and forbade you from excess. What proof could be clearer than this for their permissibility?

His saying: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ** — "and give its due on the day of its harvest." That is, the established right (الحق) pertaining to it. The pronoun refers back to the fruit, and the "due" is annexed to it because it pertains to it, just as the "due" is also annexed to the poor because it is linked to them. It is also possible that the pronoun refers to Allah, like the pronoun that follows it in His saying: **إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ** — "Indeed He does not like the wasteful," and its annexation to the Exalted would be due to its attribution to Him as His decree.

This is an indication of the decree of a certain right for the poor in fruits, grains, and produce to be given to them on the day of harvest—a matter supported by reason and endorsed by the Sharia. This is not the prescribed Zakat (الزكاة) in Islam, as Zakat is not due on everything mentioned in the verse. Furthermore, the verse is Meccan (مكية), while the ruling of Zakat is Medinan (مدني).

However, it is not unlikely that this serves as the basis for its legislation, for the foundations of the laws revealed in Medinan chapters were revealed in a general and non-specific (إجمال وإبهام) manner in Meccan chapters, such as His saying a few verses later when listing the categories of prohibitions: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ** — "Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you’" until He said: **وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ** — "and do not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward" (6:151).

His saying: **وَلا تُسْرِفُوا** — "and do not be wasteful" etc. That is, do not exceed the limit that rectifies your livelihood in your disposal of it. The owner of the wealth should not act wastefully in eating it, or squander it in giving, or spend it on what is not its proper place, such as acts of disobedience to Allah, and so on. Nor should the poor recipient be wasteful by wasting what he takes, and the like. The speech is general (إطلاق), and the address is to all people.

As for the claim of some that the address in "do not be wasteful" is specific to the owners of wealth, or the claim of others that it is directed to the Imam who collects the charity, or the claim that it means "do not be wasteful by eating it before the harvest so as not to diminish the right of the poor," or the claim that it means "do not fall short by withholding some of the obligation," or the claim that it means "do not spend it in disobedience"—all of these are refuted by the generality and the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنَ الْأَنْعامِ حَمُولَةً وَفَرْشاً** — "And of the livestock, [He created] some for burden and some for fur" until the end of the verse. Beasts of burden (الحمولة) are the larger livestock capable of carrying loads. Fur/small livestock (الفرش) are the smaller ones, either because they seem to spread (تفترش) upon the ground or because they are trodden upon like a carpet (فرش). His saying: **كُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ** — "Eat of what Allah has provided you" is a permission for eating and an endorsement of what reason dictates, similar to His saying in the previous verse: "Eat of its fruit." His saying: **لا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُواتِ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ** — "and do not follow in the footsteps of Satan. Indeed he is your manifest enemy." That is, do not proceed in this matter whose permissibility is legislated by following Satan—placing your foot where he places his—by forbidding what He has made lawful. It has been mentioned previously that following the footsteps of Satan means forbidding what Allah has made lawful without knowledge.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ مِنَ الضَّأْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ الْمَعْزِ اثْنَيْنِ** — "[He created] eight mates: two of sheep and two of goats" until the end of the verse. This is a detailed elaboration of the livestock after the general mention, intended to intensify the blame and rebuke upon them by extending it to every form and aspect. Thus, His saying: "eight mates" is an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for "some for burden and some for fur" in the previous verse.

Mates (الأزواج) is the plural of *zawj* (زوج). The term *zawj* is applied to a single entity that has another with it, and also to the pair. The types of livestock counted are four: sheep, goats, cattle, and camels. When considering the male and female of each, they become eight mates.

The meaning is: He produced eight mates: from the sheep two mates—the male and the female—and from the goats two mates like the sheep. Say: Is it the two males of the sheep and goats that Allah has forbidden, or the two females, or what the wombs of the two females of the sheep and goats contain? Inform me with knowledge, if you are truthful.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنَ الْإِبِلِ اثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ الْبَقَرِ اثْنَيْنِ** — "and two of camels and two of oxen" — until His saying — **الْأُنْثَيَيْنِ** — "the two females." Its meaning is clear from what has passed. It is also said that "two" in the four instances in the two verses refers to the domestic and the wild.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ كُنْتُمْ شُهَداءَ إِذْ وَصَّاكُمُ اللهُ بِهذا** — "Were you witnesses when Allah enjoined this upon you?" until the end of the verse. This is one side of a disjunction whose other side is omitted as indicated by the speech. Its implied meaning is: Did you know this through the path of thought, such as reason or hearing, or did you witness Allah’s prohibition and hear it directly from Him, such that you claim this?

His saying: **فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً** — "So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah" etc., is derived from what precedes it, considering it indicates their inability to answer. Accordingly, its meaning is: "Who is a greater wrongdoer than you?" The phrase "than him who fabricates" etc., is an allusion (كناية) to the polytheists being addressed, placed in stead of the second-person pronoun to indicate the reason for the judgment understood from the rhetorical question. The implied meaning is: None is a greater wrongdoer than you, because you fabricated a lie against Allah to mislead people without knowledge; and since you have done wrong, you shall not be guided, for "Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ لا أَجِدُ فِي ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلى طاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ** — "Say, ‘I do not find in what has been revealed to me anything forbidden to an eater who eats it’" etc. The meaning of the verse is clear. What was mentioned regarding the similar verse in Surah *al-Ma'idah* (verse 3) and Surah *al-Baqarah* (verse 173) is useful here.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا كُلَّ ذِي ظُفُرٍ** — "And to the Jews We forbade every animal with undivided hoof" etc. Hoof/claw (الظفر) is the singular of *azfar*, which is the bone growing at the tips of the digits. Entrails (الحوايا) refers to the intestines. It is said in *al-Majma'*: The position of "the entrails" (الحوايا) could be nominative (*raf'*), conjoined to "the backs" (الظهور), meaning: "or what the entrails carry." Or it could be accusative (*nasb*), conjoined to the "what" (ما) in His saying: "except what [their backs] carry." As for His saying: **أَوْ مَا اخْتَلَطَ بِعَظْمٍ** — "or what is mixed with bone," this "what" is conjoined to the first "what." (End of quote). The first view is closer.

He then said: "That" (ذلك) in His saying — **ذلِكَ جَزَيْناهُمْ** — "That is how We requited them" can be in the accusative position as the second object of "We requited them." The implied meaning is: "We requited them with that because of their rebellion." It cannot be in the nominative as a subject (*mubtada'*) because the implied meaning would then be "That, We requited them [with] it," which is only permissible in the necessity of poetry. (End of quote).

The verse is as if it were in the position of a rectification (استدراك) and a rebuttal of an objection, by explaining that the good things (طيبات) of what Allah provided the Children of Israel, which He forbade them, were only forbidden as a requital for their rebellion. This does not contradict their being lawful according to their primary nature, as indicated by His saying: **كُلُّ الطَّعامِ كانَ حِلًّا لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِلَّا ما حَرَّمَ إِسْرائِيلُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْراةُ** — "All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself before the Torah was sent down" (3:93), and His saying: **فَبِظُلْمٍ مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ وَبِصَدِّهِمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ كَثِيراً** — "Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We forbade them certain good things that were permitted to them, and for their barring many from the way of Allah" (4:160).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنْ كَذَّبُوكَ فَقُلْ رَبُّكُمْ ذُو رَحْمَةٍ واسِعَةٍ** — "But if they deny you, say, ‘Your Lord is of an all-embracing mercy’" until the end of the verse. The meaning of the verse is clear. It contains a command to warn and threaten them, if they deny, with the divine punishment (البأس) that cannot be turned back. However, this is not done in a way that overwhelms them with despair and hopelessness, but rather in a way mixed with some hope. For this reason, He preceded it with: "Your Lord is of an all-embracing mercy."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما أَشْرَكْنا وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "The polytheists will say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have ascribed any partner [to Him], nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything.’" The verse mentions their argument with this proof and then rebuts them by stating they are ignorant of it and only rely on conjecture (الظن) and guesswork (التخمين). The statement itself is a word of truth mentioned in many Quranic verses, but it does not yield the conclusion they intended from it.

For they only argued with it to prove that their polytheism and their prohibition of what Allah provided them were endorsed by Allah, the Glorified, and thus there was no blame upon them. Their argument was: If Allah had willed for us the opposite of the polytheism and prohibition we are upon, we would have been compelled to abandon them. Since He did not so will, it constitutes permission for polytheism and prohibition, so there is no harm in them.

This argument does not yield this result. Rather, it yields that since Allah, the Glorified, did not will that [compulsion] from them, He did not place them in a position of necessity and coercion. Thus, they are free agents (مختارون) in polytheism or refraining from it, and in prohibition or abandoning it. Therefore, He has the right to call them to faith in Him and to reject the fabrication. Thus, to Allah belongs the conclusive argument (الحجة البالغة), and they have no argument in that except following conjecture and guesswork.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ فَلِلَّهِ الْحُجَّةُ الْبالِغَةُ فَلَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "Say, ‘To Allah belongs the conclusive argument. Had He wished, He would have surely guided you all.’" It is as if the first *fa* is to derive the content of the sentence from what preceded of their saying "Had Allah wished, we would not have ascribed any partner," etc., and the second *fa* is for providing the reason (التعليل). Thus, the speech is of the nature of turning the opponent's argument against them after explaining its implication.

The meaning is that the result of the argument has become confused for you due to your ignorance, your following of conjecture, and your guesswork regarding divine knowledge. Your own argument indicates that you have no argument against His calling you to reject polytheism and cease the fabrication against Him, and that the argument belongs solely to Allah against you. For had He willed, He would have guided you all and coerced you into faith and the abandonment of polytheism and prohibition. Since He did not coerce you into that and left you to your free will, He has the right to call you to abandon polytheism and prohibition.

In other words: It follows from your argument that the argument belongs to Allah against you, because had He willed, He would have coerced [you] into faith and thus guided you all. He did not do so, but rather made you free agents, thereby making it permissible to call you to what He called you to.

Allah has explained in a group of previous verses that He did not compel His servants into faith, nor did He will that from them with a creative will (المشية التكوينية) such that they would be coerced into it. Rather, He permitted them to do the opposite. This permission—which is the removal of the creative obstacle—is the free will (اختيار) of the servants and their power over both sides of action and omission. This permission does not contradict the legislative command (الأمر التشريعي) to abandon polytheism, for example; rather, it is the foundation upon which command and prohibition are built.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ هَلُمَّ شُهَداءَكُمُ الَّذِينَ يَشْهَدُونَ** — "Say, ‘Bring your witnesses who testify’" until the end of the verse. "Bring (هلم) your witnesses" means "produce your witnesses." It is a verbal noun (*ism fi'l*) that remains the same for singular, dual, and plural. What is meant by testimony is the testimony of performance (شهادة الأداء). The indication in "this" (هذا) refers to the aforementioned prohibitions they held. The address is a challenge (تعجيزي) commanded by Allah, the Glorified, to reveal that they are fabricators in their claim that Allah forbade those things; it is an allusion to the non-existence of the prohibition.

His saying: **فَإِنْ شَهِدُوا فَلا تَشْهَدْ مَعَهُمْ** — "So if they testify, do not testify with them" is in the sense of progression (الترقي). The meaning is: There is no witness among them who can testify to that, so there is no prohibition; to the extent that even if they were to testify to the prohibition, do not testify with them, for there is no prohibition, and their testimony is not to be heeded, as they are a people who follow their desires.

Thus, His saying: **وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا** — "and do not follow the desires of those who deny Our signs" etc., is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) for His saying: "So if they testify, do not testify with them." That is, your testimony would be a following of their desires, just as their testimony is from following desires. And how could it be otherwise? For they are a people who denied the radiant signs of Allah, do not believe in the Hereafter, and equate others from His creation—such as idols—with their Lord. None would dare do such a thing, despite the perfection of the explanation and the brilliance of the proof, except those who follow their desires.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَما كانَ لِشُرَكائِهِمْ فَلا يَصِلُ إِلَى اللهِ** — "What is for their partners does not reach Allah" — he said: "Whenever what was set aside for the idols became mixed with what was set aside for Allah, the Exalted, they would return it [to the idols' share]. But if what was set aside for Allah became mixed with what was set aside for the idols, they would leave it and say: 'Allah is more self-sufficient.' If water broke through from the portion for Allah into the portion for the idols, they would not block it. But if it broke through from the portion for the idols into the portion for Allah, they would block it and say: 'Allah is more self-sufficient.'" This is narrated from Ibn 'Abbas and Qatadah, and it is what is narrated from our Imams (peace be upon them).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَذلِكَ زَيَّنَ لِكَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — "That is how [their partners] have made [the killing of their children] seem decorous to many of the polytheists" — he said: "It means their ancestors made the killing of their children seem decorous to them."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا هذِهِ أَنْعامٌ وَحَرْثٌ حِجْرٌ** — "And they say, ‘These livestock and crops are a taboo’" — he said: "Taboo (الحجر) means forbidden."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَ جَنَّاتٍ** — "It is He who produced gardens" — he said: "Gardens (الجنات) means orchards."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ وَلا تُسْرِفُوا إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ** — "and give its due on the day of its harvest, and do not be wasteful. Indeed He does not like the wasteful" — Ahmad ibn Idris informed us, saying: Ahmad ibn Muhammad narrated to us from 'Ali ibn al-Hakam from Aban ibn 'Uthman from Shu'ayb al-'Aqarqūfī, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about His saying: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ**. He said: 'A handful (الضغث) of grain and a handful (الكف) of dates when they are estimated.' He said: I asked him, 'Is it proper to give it after he has brought it into his house?' He said: 'No, he is more generous of soul before he enters his house.'"

In the same source, from Ahmad ibn Idris from al-Barqi from Sa'd ibn Sa'd from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): He was asked: "If the poor are not present while he is harvesting, what should he do?" He said: "There is nothing upon him."

In *al-Kafi*, from 'Ali ibn Ibrahim from Ibn Abi 'Umayr from Mu'awiyah ibn al-Hajjaj, who said: "I heard Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: 'Regarding crops, there are two rights: a right that is taken from you, and a right that you give.' I said: 'What is that which is taken from me, and what is that which I give?' He said: 'As for what is taken from you, it is the tithe (العشر) and the half-tithe. As for what you give, it is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ**, meaning from your harvest, one thing after another.' And I only know him to have said: 'You give a handful, then a handful, until you finish.'"

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Abu Nasr from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ وَلا تُسْرِفُوا**. He said: 'My father used to say: It is from wastefulness (الإسراف) in harvesting and picking dates that a man gives charity with both his hands together. My father, when he attended any of this and saw one of his servants giving charity with both hands, would shout at him: Give with one hand, a palmful after a palmful, and a handful after a handful of grain.'"

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Musaddif, who said: "I was with Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) on some land of his while they were harvesting dates. A beggar came to ask, so I said: 'May Allah provide for you.' He said: 'Stop! That is not for you until you have given to three. Once you have given, then it is for you [to say that], and if you withhold, it is for you.'"

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi 'Umayr from Hisham ibn al-Muthanna, who said: "A man asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ وَلا تُسْرِفُوا ـ إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ**. He said: 'There was a certain son of a certain person from the Ansar—and he named him—who had crops. When he harvested, he would give it all in charity and remain with his family with nothing. So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made that wastefulness (إسرافاً).'"

**I say:** What is meant is the application (انطباق) of the verse to his action, not its revelation concerning him, for the verse is Meccan. Perhaps the Ansari mentioned is Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas. Al-Tabari and others narrated from Ibn Jurayj, who said: "It was revealed concerning Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas; he harvested palms and said: 'No one shall come to me today but that I will feed him.' He fed people until evening and did not have a single date left. So Allah revealed: **وَلا تُسْرِفُوا إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ**." As mentioned, the verse is Meccan, not Medinan, so it does not encompass the action of Thabit ibn Qays except by way of flow and application (الجري والانطباق).

In *Tafsir al-'Ayyashi*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "Give to whoever of the Muslims is present with you. If none but a polytheist is present, then give [to him]."

**I say:** The narrations regarding these meanings from Abu Ja'far, Abu 'Abdillah, and Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon them) are very numerous.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Nahhas, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ**. He said: "What falls from the grain."

In the same source, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from Ibn 'Abbas regarding **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ**: "It was abrogated by the tithe and the half-tithe."

**I say:** The relationship between this verse and the verse of Zakat is not one of abrogation (النسخ), as there is no contradiction that leads to abrogation, whether we say the charity is obligatory or recommended.

In the same source, Abu 'Ubayd, Ibn Abi Shaybah, 'Abd ibn Humayd, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from al-Dahhak, who said: "Zakat abrogated every charity in the Quran."

**I say:** The discussion regarding this is the same as the previous one.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Maymun ibn Mihran and Yazid ibn al-Asamm, who said: "When the people of Medina harvested palms, they would bring a cluster (العذق) and place it in the mosque. A beggar would come and strike it with a stick so that [dates] would fall from it. That is His saying: **وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصادِهِ**."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ مِنَ الضَّأْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَمِنَ الْمَعْزِ اثْنَيْنِ** — "eight mates: two of sheep and two of goats" — he said: "These are what Allah made lawful in His Book in His saying: **وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ** — 'and He has sent down for you eight mates of livestock.' Then He explained them in this verse, saying: 'two of sheep, two of goats, two of camels, and two of oxen.' He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said regarding His saying: 'two of sheep,' He meant the domestic and the mountain [wild] sheep; 'and two of goats,' He meant the domestic and the wild mountain goat; 'and two of oxen,' He meant the domestic and the wild mountain ox; 'and two of camels,' He meant the Bactrian and the Arabian. These are what Allah made lawful."

**I say:** What supports this is narrated in *al-Kafi*, *al-Ikhtisas*, and *Tafsir al-'Ayyashi* from Dawud al-Raqqi and Safwan al-Jammal from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). The question remains whether the meaning of "mate" (الزوج) in the verse "eight mates: two of sheep..." is the same as in the verse "and He has sent down for you eight mates of livestock" or something else; this will come to you, Allah willing.

In *Tafsir al-'Ayyashi*, from Hariz from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): He was asked about predatory birds and beasts, until hedgehogs, bats, donkeys, mules, and horses were mentioned to him. He said: "Nothing is forbidden except what Allah has forbidden in His Book. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) forbade the eating of donkey meat on the day of Khaybar only because of their backs—lest they be depleted; donkeys are not forbidden." And he said: He read these verses: **قُلْ لا أَجِدُ فِي ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ مُحَرَّماً عَلى طاعِمٍ يَطْعَمُهُ ـ إِلَّا أَنْ يَكُونَ مَيْتَةً أَوْ دَماً مَسْفُوحاً أَوْ لَحْمَ خِنزِيرٍ**.

**I say:** There are other reports with this meaning narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them). In several of them: "The forbidden is only what Allah has forbidden in His Book, but they used to find certain things distasteful, so we find them distasteful." There are many narrations that forbid eating many animals, such as beasts with fangs and birds with talons, and others. The matter in the narrations of the Sunnis is similar. This is a jurisprudential issue whose reference is Fiqh. If the prohibition of things other than those mentioned in the verse is established, it is from what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) forbade due to their being loathsome (استخباثاً), and Allah has described him with what He endorses in his right, saying: **الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهُمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّباتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخبائِثَ** — "those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel, who bids them to do what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them from all vicious things" (7:157).

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا كُلَّ ذِي ظُفُرٍ** — "And to the Jews We forbade every animal with undivided hoof" — he said: "The kings of the Children of Israel used to prevent their poor from eating the meat of birds and fats, so Allah forbade that to them as a requital for their rebellion against their poor." This was mentioned by 'Ali ibn Ibrahim in his commentary.

In *Amali al-Shaykh*, with its chain of narration from Mas'adah ibn Ziyad, who said: "I heard Ja'far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon him) being asked about the saying of the Exalted: **فَلِلَّهِ الْحُجَّةُ الْبالِغَةُ**. He said: 'Allah, the Exalted, will say to the servant on the Day of Resurrection: My servant, were you knowledgeable? If he says: Yes, He will say to him: Why then did you not act upon what you knew? And if he says: I was ignorant, He will say: Why then did you not learn so that you might act? Thus He overcomes him in argument; that is the conclusive argument (الحجة البالغة).'"

**I say:** This is an explanation of an instance (مصداق) of the concept.
